subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
White phosphorus is composed of $P_4$ molecules with a tetrahedral structure. Each P atom is bonded to the other three P atoms by single bonds.

The reaction of white phosphorus with oxygen to form $P_4O_{10}$(s) is thermodynamically favorable at 298 K, represented by:

Equation 1: $P_4$(s) + 5 $O_2$(g) ‚Üí $P_4O_{10}$(s)

parts.prompt:
In the box in part A, complete the Lewis diagram for $P_4$ by drawing the nonbonding
electrons.

The reaction of white phosphorus with oxygen to form $P_4O_{10}$(s) is thermodynamically favorable at 298 K. The reaction is represented by equation 1.

Equation 1: $P_4$(s) + 5 $O_2$(g) ‚Üí $P_4O_{10}$(s)

i. The entropy change of the reaction, $\Delta S¬į$, is negative. Using particle-level reasoning, explain why the entropy decreases as the reaction progresses.

ii. The enthalpy change of the reaction, $\Delta H¬į$, is also negative. A student claims that the favorability of the reaction is driven by enthalpy and not by entropy. Is the student's claim correct? Justify your answer by using the relationship between $\Delta G¬į$, $\Delta H¬į$, and $\Delta S¬į$.

$P_4O_{10}$(s) reacts exothermically with water to form phosphoric acid, as represented by equation 2.

Equation 2: $P_4O_{10}$(s) + 6 $H_2O$(l) ‚Üí 4 $H_3PO_4$(aq)

required_elements: Because gas particles are more dispersed (have more microstates) than solids, the entropy

parts.prompt:
The chemist carries out the calorimetry experiment and records the following information.

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Mass of $P_4O_{10}$ | 0.100 g |
| Mass of $H_2O$ | 100.0 g |
| Initial temperature | 22.00¬įC |
| Final temperature | 22.38¬įC |
| Molar mass of $P_4O_{10}$ | 283.9 g/mol |
| Specific heat of $H_2O$ | 4.18 J/g¬∑¬įC |

i. Calculate the amount of heat, q, released during the experiment, in kJ. Assume that the specific heat of the solution is the same as that of water.

ii. Calculate the value of $\Delta H¬į_{rxn}$ for equation 2 in kJ/mol rxn. Include the sign in your answer.

scoring_points.description: For the correct sign: ‚ąí450 kJ/mol rxn

required_elements: For the correct sign: ‚ąí450 kJ/mol rxn

parts.prompt:
The chemist weighed out 0.100 g of $P_4O_{10}$ and 100.0 g of $H_2O$ to perform a second trial. In the second trial, some of the solid $P_4O_{10}$ stuck to the weighing paper and was not transferred to the calorimeter. Given that $P_4O_{10}$ is the limiting reactant, would $\Delta T$ for the second trial be greater than, less than, or equal to the value in the first trial? Justify your answer.

$P_4$(s) also reacts readily with $Cl_2$(g) to produce phosphorus trichloride, $PCl_3$(g), which in turn reacts with $Cl_2$(g) in an equilibrium process to produce $PCl_5$(g). The reactions are represented by equations 3 and 4.

Equation 3: $P_4$(s) + 6 $Cl_2$(g) ‚Üí 4 $PCl_3$(g)     $\Delta H¬į_1$ = ‚ąí1148 kJ/mol rxn
Equation 4: $PCl_3$(g) + $Cl_2$(g) ‚áĆ $PCl_5$(g)     $\Delta H¬į_2$ = ‚ąí88 kJ/mol rxn

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification: Less than. If less $P_4O_{10}$ is present, less thermal energy will be transferred to the water during the reaction, causing the temperature increase to be less than it was with 0.100 g of $P_4O_{10}$.

required_elements: For the correct answer and a valid justification: Less than. If less $P_4O_{10}$ is present, less thermal energy will be transferred to the water during the reaction, causing the temperature increase to be less than it was with 0.100 g of $P_4O_{10}$.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the standard enthalpy of formation of $PCl_5$(g) represented by equation 5.

Equation 5: $\frac{1}{4}$ $P_4$(s) + $\frac{5}{2}$ $Cl_2$(g) ‚Üí $PCl_5$(g)     $\Delta H¬į_f$ = ?\n\nThe following particle-level diagram represents the contents of the vessel in an equilibrium mixture at 546 K involving equation 4.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value. Using Hess's law: $\Delta H_{f, PCl_5(g)} = \frac{1}{4}\Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 = \frac{1}{4}(-1148) + (-88) = -375$ kJ/mol

required_elements: For the correct calculated value. Using Hess's law: $\Delta H_{f, PCl_5(g)} = \frac{1}{4}\Delta H_1 + \Delta H_2 = \frac{1}{4}(-1148) + (-88) = -375$ kJ/mol

parts.prompt:
Equation 4 for the reaction that occurs is shown.

Equation 4: $PCl_3$(g) + $Cl_2$(g) ‚áĆ $PCl_5$(g)     $\Delta H¬į_2$ = ‚ąí88 kJ/mol rxn

i. If each particle in the diagram represents a partial pressure of 1.00 atm, what is the value of $K_p$ for the equilibrium mixture at 546 K?

ii. Does the value of $K_p$ increase, decrease, or remain the same when the temperature is increased to 596 K? Justify your answer based on $\Delta H¬į$.

alternatives.correct_example: Decrease. The negative value of $\Delta H¬į$ indicates that the reaction is exothermic. Because the forward reaction is exothermic, increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium toward reactants, decreasing $K_p$.
